1: sēmeion allo en tō ouranō mega kai thaumaston: another great and marvelous sign in heaven. Thaumaston (marvelous, wonderful) — the same word used for the works of God in v. 3. The seven last plagues complete (etelesthē — aorist passive, proleptic announcement: it stands completed) the wrath of God. Etelesthē is the same root as Jesus' tetelestai ("it is finished," John 19:30) — the completion of wrath corresponds to the completion of atonement. The seven bowls are the final expression of divine judgment, bookending history with the cross.
2: thalassan hyalinēn memigmenēn pyri: a sea of glass (thalassa hyalinē, 4:6) now mixed with fire (memigmenēn pyri). In 4:6 the sea of glass was crystal-clear, before the throne, symbolizing divine transcendence and purity. Now mixed with fire — the community of overcomers stands on it, having passed through the fire of tribulation. Kithāras tou theou: harps of God — instruments of praise (Ps 33:2; 150:3; 1 Chr 25:6). The victors stand (hestōtas) in triumph with the instrument of worship.
3: ōdēn Mōyseōs tou doulou tou theou kai tēn ōdēn tou Arniou: the song of Moses the servant of God and the song of the Lamb — a unified song, not two songs. The Song of Moses (Exod 15) celebrated Israel's deliverance through the Red Sea; the Song of the Lamb celebrates the new exodus through the great tribulation. Both deliverances are celebrated as one: the same God who split the sea saves his people from every beast. The content is a mosaic of OT texts: Megala kai thaumasta ta erga sou, Kyrie ho theos ho Pantokratōr (Ps 111:2; 139:14) — Dikaiai kai alēthinai hai hodoi sou (Deut 32:4; Ps 145:17).
4: Tis ou mē phobēthē, Kyrie, kai doxasei to onoma sou: "Who will not fear you, O Lord, and glorify your name?" — the rhetorical question from Jer 10:7 (Who would not fear you, O King of the nations? For this is your due). Hoti monos hosios: for you alone are holy (monos hosios) — a claim that none of the beast's divine titles can match. Panta ta ethnē hēxousin kai proskunēsousin enōpion sou: all nations will come and worship before you — the eschatological universalism of Ps 86:9; Isa 66:23; Zeph 2:11. The song anticipates the fulfillment before the bowls that will bring it about.
5: ho naos tēs skēnēs tou martyriou en tō ouranō ēnoigē: the temple of the tabernacle of testimony in heaven was opened. Skēnē tou martyriou is specifically the wilderness tabernacle — the tent of meeting that housed the ark of the covenant with the tablets of the law (testimony). The opening of the heavenly tabernacle reveals its contents: the seven angels with the seven plagues emerge from inside. The judgment proceeds from within the holy place — from the law's demands being met by the wrath that the law prescribes for covenant violation.
6: endedymenoi linon katharon lampron kai periezōsmenoi peri ta stēthē zōnas chrysas: clothed in pure bright linen with golden sashes. The priestly garments echo Dan 10:5 (the heavenly man clothed in linen with a belt of fine gold). The seven angels are priest-warriors: they come from the temple (v. 6) dressed in priestly regalia to pour out judgment. The phialai (bowls) they carry are themselves priestly vessels — wide, shallow libation bowls used in temple worship. The judgment has a liturgical character: it is the divine worship service of holy war.
7: phialas chrysas gemas tou thymou tou theou tou zōntos eis tous aiōnas tōn aiōnōn: golden bowls (phialai) full of the wrath (thymos) of the God who lives forever and ever. Phialē is a wide, shallow bowl used for libations — the shape signals that the contents will be poured quickly and completely, not slowly dispensed. The wrath belongs to the living God (tou zōntos eis tous aiōnas) — in contrast to the dead idols of the beast-cult. The living God's wrath is executed by the living God: it has eschatological permanence and moral authority.
8: eplēsthē ho naos kapnou ek tēs doxēs tou theou kai ek tēs dynameōs autou: the temple was filled with smoke from the glory of God and from his power. This is the smoke-of-Theophany from Isa 6:4 (the house was filled with smoke at YHWH's appearance) and Exod 40:34-35 (Moses was unable to enter the tent of meeting because the cloud settled on it and the glory of the LORD filled the tabernacle). The divine presence overwhelms the temple — making it inaccessible. Oudeis edynato eiselthein eis ton naon: no one could enter until the seven plagues were completed. The theology of holy war: God fights alone; no human intercession is possible or needed during the final judgment. The closed temple = the completion of the age of intercession.
